description: AI-generated negotiation dialogues grounded in the Getting to Yes framework (Fisher, Ury & Patton, 1981/2011). Each scenario includes full dialogue annotated with GTY principles, negotiation tactics, BATNA, underlying interests, and outcome analysis. Suitable for NLP research, negotiation AI training, and educational tools.
source_framework: Getting to Yes: Negotiating Agreement Without Giving In (Fisher, Ury & Patton)
gty_principles_used: Separate the People from the Problem

scenarios.title: Software Engineer Salary Negotiation
scenarios.context: A software engineer with 4 years of experience is negotiating a salary increase during annual review. The manager has budget constraints but wants to retain the employee.
scenarios.batna_a: Has a competing offer from another company for $115,000
scenarios.batna_b: Can hire a junior engineer for $80,000 but will lose institutional knowledge
scenarios.stated_positions_a: I want a 20% raise, bringing my salary to $108,000
scenarios.stated_positions_b: The budget only allows for a 5% raise this cycle
scenarios.final_terms: $98,000 salary + $5,000 signing bonus + extra PTO + remote work flexibility

dialogue.speaker: Alex Chen
dialogue.utterance: Sarah, I appreciate you making time for my review. I've been reflecting on the past year and I'd like to discuss my compensation. Based on my contributions to the platform rewrite and the market rate for engineers with my skills, I believe a move to $108,000 is warranted.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Alex opens with a justification (market rate, contributions) rather than just stating a demand — grounding the position in objective criteria.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Kim
dialogue.utterance: Alex, I really value your work — the platform rewrite was exceptional. I want to be transparent: I'm working with a tight budget this cycle, and the approved band is 5%. That would put you at $90,300. I want to find something that works for both of us, though.
dialogue.gty_principle: Separate the People from the Problem
dialogue.gty_annotation: Sarah validates Alex personally while framing budget as a separate constraint — not a personal refusal. This keeps the relationship intact.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Chen
dialogue.utterance: I understand budget cycles are real. Can I share something? I've been approached by another company offering $115,000. I'd genuinely prefer to stay — I believe in this team's mission — but I have to make a responsible decision. I'm not using that offer as a threat; I just want you to understand my situation.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Alex reveals the BATNA honestly, not as a threat but as context — consistent with principled negotiation's emphasis on transparency and legitimate leverage.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Kim
dialogue.utterance: That's really helpful to know, and I appreciate you telling me directly. Let me think beyond just base salary for a moment. What matters most to you — is it purely the number, or are there other things like flexibility, growth path, or recognition that are important?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Sarah skillfully pivots to exploring underlying interests rather than fighting over the numbers — the heart of principled negotiation.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Chen
dialogue.utterance: Honestly? The number matters, but so does feeling valued. Remote flexibility two days a week would mean a lot for my work-life balance. And I'd love a clearer path to senior engineer — I've been doing senior-level work for over a year.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Alex expands the negotiable pie by revealing non-salary interests — creating room for creative options that could satisfy both sides.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Kim
dialogue.utterance: Here's what I can put together: base salary of $98,000, a one-time retention bonus of $5,000 paid now, two remote days per week, and I'll formally nominate you for the senior engineer track with a target promotion review in six months. I can't match $115,000 today, but I believe this package is competitive when you factor everything in.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Sarah constructs a package that addresses Alex's stated interests (salary, recognition, flexibility, growth) rather than just splitting the salary difference. Classic mutual-gain option.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Chen
dialogue.utterance: That's a thoughtful offer. The promotion timeline is actually really motivating — can we put the six-month review in writing as part of my performance plan? If I hit the criteria, I'd expect the senior title and a corresponding salary adjustment.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Alex asks for written, objective criteria for promotion — ensuring the agreement is durable and based on standards, not goodwill.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Kim
dialogue.utterance: Absolutely. I'll document the promotion criteria in your performance plan by end of week. Do we have a deal?
dialogue.gty_principle: Separate the People from the Problem
dialogue.gty_annotation: Sarah closes warmly, reinforcing the relationship. Agreement is reached without either side losing face.

analysis.notes: Classic Getting to Yes case: expanding beyond salary to include non-monetary interests created a deal that neither party could have reached by haggling over the number alone.

scenarios.title: Apartment Lease Renewal Negotiation
scenarios.context: A tenant wants to renew but rent is being raised 18%. The tenant has lived there 3 years and the landlord fears vacancy.
scenarios.batna_a: Comparable apartment 10 min away for $200 less, but moving costs $1,500
scenarios.batna_b: Average vacancy period in this market is 6 weeks; new tenant brings risk
scenarios.stated_positions_a: I won't pay more than a 5% increase
scenarios.stated_positions_b: The new rate is $2,200, up from $1,870. Non-negotiable.
scenarios.negotiation_style: mixed — starts positional, moves to principled
scenarios.final_terms: $2,000/month with 2-year lease, tenant handles minor repairs up to $100

dialogue.speaker: Maria Santos
dialogue.utterance: Mr. Park, I received the renewal notice. An 18% increase is really steep. I've been a reliable tenant for three years — never missed rent, kept the place in great shape. I can't accept $2,200.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Maria opens with a positional refusal but anchors it in objective merit (3-year track record) — partially principled but still position-focused.

dialogue.speaker: David Park
dialogue.utterance: Maria, I appreciate your tenancy. But my costs have gone up — insurance, property tax, maintenance. Every comparable unit in this zip code is going for $2,200 or more right now.
dialogue.gty_annotation: David introduces market comparables as objective criteria — a legitimate standard, though used defensively to justify a position rather than explore interests.

dialogue.speaker: Maria Santos
dialogue.utterance: Can I ask what your biggest concern is? Is it the monthly amount, or is it something else — like making sure the unit is taken care of and rent comes on time?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Maria demonstrates excellent principled negotiation by shifting the conversation to interests — a turning point in the negotiation.

dialogue.speaker: David Park
dialogue.utterance: Honestly? My worst nightmare is a bad tenant or six weeks of vacancy. You've been wonderful — I'd be foolish not to acknowledge that. I just can't leave money on the table either.
dialogue.gty_annotation: David reveals his true interests — reliability over maximum rent — which opens the door to a creative deal.

dialogue.speaker: Maria Santos
dialogue.utterance: What if we did a two-year lease at $2,000 per month? You get the security of a reliable tenant locked in for two years with zero vacancy risk — that's worth at least $2,000 in avoided costs. And I handle any minor repairs under $100 myself, saving you calls.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Maria creates a package deal grounded in David's interests (vacancy risk, maintenance hassle) — mutual gain through a creative option.

dialogue.speaker: David Park
dialogue.utterance: The two-year lock-in does help my planning a lot. And if you're taking minor repairs off my plate that saves real time. I'll do $2,000 flat with a two-year term and the repair arrangement in writing. Deal?
dialogue.gty_annotation: David accepts the reframed deal because it serves his underlying interests better than the original position would have.

analysis.notes: Negotiation started positional but turned principled when Maria asked David about his underlying interests. The multi-year lease reframed price as a risk-management exchange.

scenarios.title: Software Vendor Contract Negotiation
scenarios.context: A startup is negotiating a SaaS contract with a vendor. The vendor has a standard enterprise price; the startup needs flexibility.
scenarios.batna_a: Open-source alternative — free but requires 3 months of engineering time to implement
scenarios.batna_b: Lose the deal entirely; this is a new market segment CloudCore wants to enter
scenarios.stated_positions_a: We need your platform for under $2,000/month
scenarios.stated_positions_b: Standard pricing starts at $4,500/month for your usage tier
scenarios.final_terms: $2,400/month for Year 1 (startup discount), $3,200 Year 2, in exchange for public case study and logo usage

dialogue.speaker: Priya Nair
dialogue.utterance: James, we love the product — it solves a real pain point. But $4,500 a month is just not in our budget at our stage. We're a 20-person startup. We'd need to be under $2,000 to make this work.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Priya gives context for her position (startup stage) which begins to signal interests, but is still framed as a positional limit.

dialogue.speaker: James Okafor
dialogue.utterance: I hear you, Priya. Before we talk numbers, help me understand your roadmap. Where do you see your user base in 12 months? 24 months?
dialogue.gty_annotation: James deflects from positional haggling and gathers interest/future data — setting up a longer-term value proposition.

dialogue.speaker: Priya Nair
dialogue.utterance: We're projecting 5x growth in 18 months. If we hit our Series A, this could be a major platform for us — we'd probably be at the $4,000–$5,000 tier naturally by then. Right now we just need to survive Year 1.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Priya reveals the real interest: survival/bridge to growth, not permanent discount. This creates a path for James.

dialogue.speaker: James Okafor
dialogue.utterance: Here's what I'm thinking. CloudCore has been wanting to build credibility in the startup world. If we could feature TechStart as a case study — a written post and logo on our site — that has real value for us. Would you be open to a startup pricing arrangement in exchange for that?
dialogue.gty_annotation: James introduces a non-monetary value exchange — public reference — that costs Priya little but is worth a lot to CloudCore. Mutual gain.

dialogue.speaker: Priya Nair
dialogue.utterance: A case study is doable if the product delivers — which I believe it will. What number does that unlock?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Priya accepts the logic of the exchange and moves to specifics — deal is now on cooperative footing.

dialogue.speaker: James Okafor
dialogue.utterance: I can offer $2,400 for Year 1 — that's our official startup tier — with a scheduled move to $3,200 in Year 2 as you scale. The case study and logo rights are part of the agreement. This gets you live fast, and us into a new segment. I think it's a fair deal.
dialogue.gty_annotation: James proposes a structured pricing ladder tied to objective business growth — fair, transparent, and reciprocal.

dialogue.speaker: Priya Nair
dialogue.utterance: That works. One ask: can we include a 30-day exit clause in Year 1 in case the implementation doesn't go smoothly? That's a standard risk protection for us.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Priya invokes a standard industry practice (exit clause) as objective criteria — not a personal demand but a reasonable protection.

dialogue.speaker: James Okafor
dialogue.utterance: Fair enough. I'll add a 30-day implementation exit clause. Welcome to CloudCore, Priya.
dialogue.gty_principle: Separate the People from the Problem
dialogue.gty_annotation: James closes warmly — the problem was solved without harming the new relationship. Both parties feel good.

analysis.notes: The case study exchange is the creative linchpin — both parties found value outside the price dimension, enabling a deal below CloudCore's floor by compensating with non-monetary value.

scenarios.title: Team Resource Allocation Dispute
scenarios.context: Two department heads are competing for the same two engineers for Q3. One needs them for a client deadline, the other for an internal infrastructure project.
scenarios.batna_a: Hire a contractor for $15,000 — expensive, risky ramp-up time
scenarios.batna_b: Delay infrastructure project by one quarter — acceptable but painful
scenarios.stated_positions_a: I need both engineers for all of Q3
scenarios.stated_positions_b: Both engineers are committed to the infrastructure project — it was planned first
scenarios.final_terms: One engineer full-time to Tom for 6 weeks; second engineer split 60/40 for the quarter; infrastructure project shifted 3 weeks

dialogue.speaker: Tom Rivera
dialogue.utterance: Lena, I have a client with a contractual SLA deadline in eight weeks. If I miss it, we're looking at $200K in penalties and potentially losing a $1M account. I need both engineers.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Tom leads with the business consequence rather than just the demand — grounding his position in legitimate, verifiable stakes.

dialogue.speaker: Lena Fischer
dialogue.utterance: Tom, I hear you. But this infrastructure project was approved at the beginning of the year. If we delay again, the team's morale will tank — they've already postponed twice. And the tech debt is causing five hours of lost productivity per week across the company.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Lena counters with her own legitimate interests — team morale, company-wide productivity — equally valid and measurable.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Rivera
dialogue.utterance: I don't want to dismiss your project — what specifically in the infrastructure work is most critical to get done this quarter versus what could shift?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Tom seeks to understand the priority within Lena's interest — a classic principled move that creates room for disaggregation.

dialogue.speaker: Lena Fischer
dialogue.utterance: The database migration is non-negotiable — it's a security risk. The API refactor can be phased. So technically, one engineer could start the migration, while the second could help you for the first six weeks, then join migration work.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Lena disaggregates her own project into critical vs. flexible work — this is the kind of creative option-generation that expands possibilities.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Rivera
dialogue.utterance: That could work. What if Engineer A goes full-time to my project for weeks 1–6, then transitions back to infrastructure? And Engineer B does 60% my project, 40% migration during Q3?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Tom proposes a concrete time-sharing model — a specific mutual-gain option neither party had articulated before the conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Lena Fischer
dialogue.utterance: I can work with that if we document it clearly so the engineers aren't caught in the middle. And I want a formal commitment that they're released back to infrastructure by week 7 — no extensions without a new negotiation.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Lena insists on written terms and defined boundaries — using objective criteria (documented timelines) to protect both parties.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Rivera
dialogue.utterance: Deal. I'll send a written summary to both of us and CC our VP to make it official. And Lena — thanks for being flexible. I know this wasn't easy.
dialogue.gty_principle: Separate the People from the Problem
dialogue.gty_annotation: Tom closes with personal acknowledgment — reinforcing the relationship. The problem was solved while the relationship between two colleagues was maintained.

analysis.notes: The turning point was Lena disaggregating her own project needs — showing that not all of the infrastructure work was equally time-sensitive. This is a textbook example of expanding the zone of agreement by exploring interests rather than defending positions.

scenarios.title: Used Car Price Negotiation
scenarios.context: A buyer is negotiating the price of a used car at a dealership. The listed price is $18,500.
scenarios.batna_a: A comparable car at another dealer for $17,200, but it's 30 miles away and has slightly higher mileage
scenarios.batna_b: Car has been on the lot for 45 days — floor plan costs accumulating
scenarios.negotiation_style: mixed — positional with some principled elements

dialogue.speaker: Kevin O'Brien
dialogue.utterance: I like the car. I've done some research — KBB shows the private party value at $16,200 and the comparable at AutoWorld down the road is listed at $17,200. I'd like to offer $16,500.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Kevin leads with objective market data (KBB, competitor listing) — anchoring the offer in external standards rather than arbitrary preference.

dialogue.speaker: Rosa Mendez
dialogue.utterance: I appreciate the research. Our car has lower mileage than the AutoWorld one by 8,000 miles and it just passed our 50-point inspection. That's worth a premium. I can come down to $17,800 and that's already below what we have in it.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Rosa counters with objective differentiators (lower mileage, inspection) — also using criteria, but to justify a higher price.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin O'Brien
dialogue.utterance: The lower mileage is fair to consider. At 8,000 miles less, that's maybe $400–600 more by standard depreciation curves. So I'd say $16,900 is more defensible. What does closing this deal today mean for you?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Kevin acknowledges Rosa's point and quantifies it — then pivots to ask about her interest in timing, a principled move.

dialogue.speaker: Rosa Mendez
dialogue.utterance: Between us — this car has been here 45 days. I'd really like to move it. What would it take to get you to sign today?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Rosa reveals her interest (aged inventory, close today) — a candid moment that validates Kevin's leverage and opens room for creative resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin O'Brien
dialogue.utterance: Meet me at $17,100 and throw in a year of roadside assistance. That's a package I can shake hands on right now.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Kevin creates a package by adding a low-cost item (roadside assistance) to sweeten a price slightly above his floor — mutual gain.

dialogue.speaker: Rosa Mendez
dialogue.utterance: Roadside assistance costs me $80 wholesale. $17,100 plus that? You've got a deal. Let me get the paperwork.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Rosa accepts because the roadside assistance is low cost to her but high perceived value to Kevin — a mutual gain creative option.

analysis.notes: A largely positional negotiation elevated by both parties using objective criteria (KBB, mileage depreciation, aging inventory cost). The roadside assistance sweetener resolved the gap at low cost — a small but effective mutual-gain creative option.

scenarios.title: Community Dispute: Library Hours vs. Youth Center Noise
scenarios.context: A library and a youth community center share a building. The library wants quiet evenings; the youth center runs loud programs from 5–8pm.
scenarios.batna_a: File a formal noise complaint with the city — damages relationship and may take 6 months
scenarios.batna_b: Move evening programs to a church hall 2 miles away — inconvenient for participants
scenarios.stated_positions_a: No amplified sound after 5pm in the shared wing
scenarios.stated_positions_b: Our programs run until 8pm and we have a right to use the space
scenarios.final_terms: Acoustic panels installed (cost split), loud programs moved to east wing, quiet study hour 6–7pm library expansion into shared foyer

dialogue.speaker: Helen Zhao
dialogue.utterance: Marcus, I want to find a way forward that works for both of us. I'm not trying to shut down your programs — they're genuinely important for the neighborhood. But I'm losing adult patrons in the evenings because of the noise bleed. Can we talk about what's driving each of our concerns?
dialogue.gty_principle: Separate the People from the Problem
dialogue.gty_annotation: Helen opens by affirming the relationship and Marcus's mission — separating the person from the problem before engaging with it.

dialogue.speaker: Marcus Webb
dialogue.utterance: I appreciate that. Honestly, our biggest challenge is that many of our kids can't get here before 5pm because of school. The 5–8pm window is when we can actually reach them. The music and games — that's engagement strategy, not just noise.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Marcus explains the reasoning behind his position — revealing that the timing and engagement format are driven by the youth's constraints, not preference.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Zhao
dialogue.utterance: That makes a lot of sense. What if we thought about this physically rather than temporally? I wonder if moving the louder activities to the east wing — which shares fewer walls with our reading rooms — would reduce the bleed significantly.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Helen shifts from time-based to space-based solution — a creative reframe that doesn't require either party to give up their core schedule.

dialogue.speaker: Marcus Webb
dialogue.utterance: The east wing would work for most things. The main concern is acoustic panels — we'd need some soundproofing between the wings. That costs money neither of us has individually.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Marcus surfaces a shared implementation obstacle — which immediately reframes both parties as having a common problem to solve together.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Zhao
dialogue.utterance: What if we co-apply for a city facilities improvement grant? I know the neighborhood council has a small fund for exactly this kind of shared-space upgrade. We could split the application effort and share the panels.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Helen invents a third-party resource option — neither party bears the cost alone, and the grant application itself becomes a collaborative act.

dialogue.speaker: Marcus Webb
dialogue.utterance: I love that idea. And you know, we've been wanting more study space for our older teens during quiet hours. What if we offered the library use of our east foyer between 6 and 7pm — a quiet study overflow zone for both communities?
dialogue.gty_annotation: Marcus generates an additional mutual gain — his foyer as library overflow creates shared community value and deepens cooperation.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Zhao
dialogue.utterance: That's actually wonderful. We'd both benefit from that, and it tells the city council a great story when we apply for the grant. I think we've found something genuinely better than what either of us came in with.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Helen recognizes the outcome surpasses both parties' original asks — the hallmark of a well-executed principled negotiation.

analysis.notes: A near-perfect GTY outcome: both parties left better off than their opening positions. The shift from time-based to space-based framing was the key creative move. The joint grant application transformed competitors into collaborators.

scenarios.title: Freelance Designer Rate Dispute (Breakdown)
scenarios.context: A freelance designer is negotiating a rate increase with a long-term client. The negotiation breaks down due to positional bargaining on both sides.
scenarios.batna_a: Take on new clients at higher rates — realistic but takes time
scenarios.batna_b: Use offshore design service at $25/hour — quality risk but cheaper
scenarios.stated_positions_a: I'm raising my rate from $65 to $95/hour effective next month
scenarios.stated_positions_b: That's a 46% jump. I'll pay $70/hour and that's final.
scenarios.zone_of_possible_agreement: $78–$85/hour was reachable, but neither party explored it

dialogue.speaker: Jordan Lee
dialogue.utterance: Greg, I need to let you know that my rates are going up to $95/hour starting next month. The market rate for senior UX has risen a lot and I've invested heavily in new skills.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Jordan delivers an ultimatum without exploring interests first — classic positional opening that puts Greg on the defensive.

dialogue.speaker: Greg Hanson
dialogue.utterance: That's a 46% increase, Jordan. That's not going to happen. I can go to $70 but that's as far as I'll stretch.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Greg responds with his own positional counter — no exploration of interests, no curiosity about Jordan's reasoning. Classic positional escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Jordan Lee
dialogue.utterance: I've been with you for three years. $70 doesn't respect that history or what I bring to your brand.
dialogue.gty_principle: Separate the People from the Problem
dialogue.gty_annotation: Jordan introduces the relationship emotionally — but as a bargaining chip rather than genuinely separating people from the problem. This muddies the negotiation.

dialogue.speaker: Greg Hanson
dialogue.utterance: I respect the history. But I have offshore options at $30/hour. I'm choosing to pay more because I value consistency. $70 is fair.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Greg invokes the offshore threat as leverage — a dirty tactic of artificial alternatives designed to pressure rather than problem-solve.

dialogue.speaker: Jordan Lee
dialogue.utterance: Then maybe you should use them. I'm not dropping below $90.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Jordan digs into a new, higher floor out of anger — positions have actually diverged as a result of positional bargaining. The breakdown is now likely.

dialogue.speaker: Greg Hanson
dialogue.utterance: Fine. I'll explore other options. This conversation is over.
dialogue.gty_annotation: Negotiation collapses. Neither party explored interests, generated options, or used objective criteria. A deal in the $78–85 range was likely achievable.

analysis.notes: A textbook Getting to Yes failure case. Neither party asked 'why?' about the other's position. Jordan never asked what Greg's budget constraints were; Greg never asked what was driving Jordan's rate increase. The offshore threat was a dirty tactic that escalated rather than resolved the conflict. A three-year relationship ended over a gap that principled negotiation would likely have bridged.
